query: Kimi K3 complaints OR frustrating OR broken

results.title:
Kimi K3 dropped this week and the timeline lost its mind. I've been testing it. Here's my honest read, plus the model that actually earned a place in my week, which wasn't the one I expected.

First,… | Shane Spencer | 16 comments
results.content:
Kimi K3 dropped this week and the timeline lost its mind. I've been testing it. Here's my honest read, plus the model that actually earned a place in my week, which wasn't the one I expected.
First, K3. Big model, big context window, early testers saying it trades blows with the best coding models out there. I've been running it through real work rather than benchmarks.
So far: it's fine. Competent, nothing broken, nothing that's made me want to move a workflow over. One week isn't a verdict, so I'll keep pushing on it and report back. But fine is a long way from change your stack.
The surprise is what I've actually been reaching for. Grok 4.5 in Cursor has quietly become my default, and I didn't see that coming. [...] Aditya Somani, graphic

Token efficiency matters alot with growing time, if you see that then k3 takes 2x the tokens comapred to sol. Hence it's cost is approx. Similar but for claude(opus) it flips the thing as it takes 1/3 to 1/5 the tokens. So, it's kinda winning really great on claude side.

Jonathan Osburg, graphic

Grok is amazingly fast and smart. K3 is also fast, but in my experience simply stopped working repeatedly mid-task yesterday which was annoying.
Both very interesting to play with, time will tell which one will earn it's seat in my LLLineup :)

Naman Arora, graphic

Model is not product now days

Raymond H., graphic [...] No alternative text description for this image

Shane Spencer, graphic

For anyone wondering what "real work" means here: I'm not running benchmarks. I'm pointing these at the same builds I'd be doing anyway, multi-file changes in an existing codebase, the debugging that takes actual context, the unglamorous stuff.
That's the only test that tells you anything. A model can look brilliant on a clean problem and fall apart the second it has to hold a messy repo in its head.
K3 gets another week. If it changes my mind, I'll say so here.

Jose Luis Flores® ☁, graphic

I tried Grok 4.5 yesterday. I didn't like the output as much as Opus 4.8 High, to be quite honest, and Fable actually beat Grok 4.5 too. Building abs currently, but I don't mind trying Grok again.

Alex Cinovoj, graphic

results.title: Kimi K3 Review: Benchmarks, Pricing, and K2 Comparison
results.content:
Share:

The largest open-weight model ever announced is Chinese, costs 5x more than its predecessor, and posted the best browsing-agent score ever published, all in the same week. Kimi K3 landed on July 16, 2026, and it broke the one rule every Chinese AI lab had followed until now: it is not trying to be the cheap alternative. At $3 input and $15 output per million tokens, Moonshot AI is charging Claude Sonnet money and claiming frontier results to justify it. [...] What Is Kimi K3?
Kimi K3 is a 2.8 trillion parameter Mixture-of-Experts multimodal model from Moonshot AI, released July 16, 2026, with a 1 million token context window, always-on reasoning with a tunable reasoning_effort control, and input support for text, images, and video. It is live in the Kimi app, the Playground, and the API, with open weights promised by July 27, 2026. VentureBeat called it the largest open-source model ever announced, and on raw parameter count nothing public comes close. [...] Test 1: Agentic Web Research
The BrowseComp record feels earned. I gave K3 a genuinely nasty research task: reconstruct the pricing history of four AI models across 2026 from primary sources, with citations. It ran a long chain of web searches, cross-checked conflicting reports, and produced a sourced timeline that matched my own records on 11 of 12 data points. The one miss cited a rumor as confirmed. Against the same task, GPT-5.6 Sol was faster but shallower, checking fewer sources. For agent-style research, K3 is the best I have tested, full stop.
Test 2: Coding Against K2.7 Code

results.title: Kimi K3 (Fully Tested): AN OPEN MODEL BEATS FABLE?!
results.content: that the model works way better with Kimmy CLI. It's very much optimized for that. I tried it in a couple of other harnesses and it works fine, but the difference with Kimmy CLI is noticeable. So, if you want to get the best out of this model when it launches, I'd recommend using it through Kimmy CLI. So, what's the verdict? If you ask me, Kimmy K3 is the real deal. It's third on my leaderboard behind Fable 5 and Opus 4.8, but in real agentic work, it feels better than Opus 4.8 and GPT 5.6 Soul. The long horizon performance, the tool usage, and the fact that it doesn't overthink make it one of the most practical models I have tested. If Moonshot prices this anywhere near their previous models, this is going to be a really good option for sure. Again, I don't know yet if it's going to be [...] anything special up for that. It just figured out that the tool was there and used it. Most models don't do this unless you explicitly tell them to. Second, it doesn't give up. Like I said before, it grabs a task by its head and keeps working until it's actually done on the Gemmauning task. It hit multiple errors and just worked through all of them without asking me anything. Third, it doesn't overthink. This is a really underrated point. A lot of the recent reasoning models will burn through thousands of tokens thinking about a simple task before doing anything. K3 just thinks the right amount and gets to work. It makes the model feel fast and also keeps the costs down, which is always a big deal for me. One more thing to note is that the model works way better with Kimmy CLI. It's very [...] works. The elevator logic is correct. People wait properly and get picked up one by one, and the animations are smooth. Only some minor polish issues keep it from a perfect score. For reference, Opus 148 got a 10 on this one and Fable 5 got a nine. So K3 is right up there with the top models. This is pretty amazing to be honest. The second question is the 3JS contact lens case. It should be a 3D model of a contact lens case with pronounced L and R caps. And you should be able to click on the caps to open them up. Let's send it and check. So here it gets a 5 out of 10. The model is there, the caps are labeled, but the opening interaction is a bit janky. To be fair, this is a test where almost every model struggles. Fable 5 is the only one that really nailed it with a 10, and Opus 4.8 got a

results.title: Kimi K3 and Inkling: The Frontier Got Crowded
results.content: We ran Bonsai 27B on the lab hardware. Here are the numbers. Yesterday’s top story ended with a promise, and the benchmarks went up on the blog today: Bonsai’s 1-bit (3.79GB) and ternary (7.17GB) builds, head to head on the Utho-sponsored RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell (96GB, ~1.8TB/s) and a DGX Spark (128GB unified, 273GB/s). The RTX generates 145.5 tokens per second on the 1-bit build and 120.7 on ternary, and speculative decoding lifts code generation from 117 to 156. The Spark does 42.8 and 28.5, still fully interactive, but speculative decoding actually hurts it, dropping to 17.6, so measure before you optimize. Quality-wise nothing looked broken in smoke tests: the 1-bit build reasons correctly on arithmetic and JSON but burns its token budget on harder coding tasks, while ternary stayed

results.title: Kimi K3 Is Here to Topple GPT and Claude. Is It That Good?
results.content:
Early hands-on reaction has been mixed, as it tends to be with any major launch. One of the first detailed posts on r/kimi noted: "The Kimi K3 is good... but it's not that much better than the Kimi K2.7," and flagged something worth tracking at scale, quota use, noting it "consumes about 6% of the weekly quota per hour" under sustained load, useful context for teams running agentic workloads rather than one-off chats. [...] ## Tips for Prompting Kimi Models Well

Today's live session put these habits to the test on something visual: a flight-tracking globe rebuilt piece by piece against K3, following the same file-by-file rhythm Moonshot's own docs recommend for the K2 lineage. Geometry came first, then the earth sphere with its atmosphere and stars, then a live data feed, then the render loop, then the interactive layer, then one final pass wiring all five pieces together, with each file checked on its own before the model touched the next one, since an agentic model this capable will quietly paper over a broken dependency rather than flag it, and that single habit is worth keeping long after the demo ends.
